meta.description: Compressed hunt cards for general-governance — one-line detection rules for bulk scanning
meta.usage: Load cards into context. For each card, grep target code for card.grep. On hit: execute card.check steps directly, use validWhen/invalidWhen/impact for rapid triage, use optional preconditions/proofShape/falsePositiveSignals/reportEvidence when present, and only read full DB entry (card.ref + card.lines) for confirmed true/likely positives.

cards.title: 1. Flash Loan Governance Takeover
cards.detect: When governance voting power is determined by token balances at the time of vote (snapshot-less) or uses LP tokens that...
check: VERIFY: When governance voting power is determined by token balances at the time of vote (snapshot-less) or uses LP tokens that can be acquired via flash...
PROVE: Pre-stage: submit a malicious proposal with an innocent-looking description; Wait for minimum proposal delay (e.g., 1 day for Beanstalk)
ANTIPATTERN: emergencyCommit allows same-tx proposal execution
cards.antipattern: emergencyCommit allows same-tx proposal execution
cards.validWhen: When governance voting power is determined by token balances at the time of vote (snapshot-less) or uses LP tokens that can be acquired via flash loans, an attacker...
preconditions: governance voting power is determined by token balances at the time of vote (snapshot-less) or uses LP tokens that can be...
Deposit LP tokens into governance silo for voting power
Malicious proposal executes: sweeps treasury to attacker
cards.proofShape: Pre-stage: submit a malicious proposal with an innocent-looking description -> Wait for minimum proposal delay (e.g., 1 day for Beanstalk) -> Take massive flash loans (stablecoins, ETH)

cards.title: 2. Low Quorum Governance Exploitation
cards.detect: When governance quorum requirements are set too low (or vote delegation concentrates power), an attacker with a...
check: VERIFY: When governance quorum requirements are set too low (or vote delegation concentrates power), an attacker with a relatively small token holding can...
PROVE: Low quorum allows malicious proposal to pass
cards.antipattern: Low quorum allows malicious proposal to pass
cards.validWhen: When governance quorum requirements are set too low (or vote delegation concentrates power), an attacker with a relatively small token holding can pass proposals that...
preconditions: governance quorum requirements are set too low (or vote delegation concentrates power), an attacker with a relatively small token...
cards.proofShape: Low quorum allows malicious proposal to pass

cards.title: 3. Proxy Re-Initialization Attack
cards.detect: When governance, staking, and delegate manager contracts are deployed behind proxies (UUPS or Transparent), the...
check: VERIFY: When governance, staking, and delegate manager contracts are deployed behind proxies (UUPS or Transparent), the initialize() function must be called...
PROVE: governance proxy initialize() can be called again
cards.antipattern: governance proxy initialize() can be called again
cards.validWhen: When governance, staking, and delegate manager contracts are deployed behind proxies (UUPS or Transparent), the initialize() function must be called exactly once and...
preconditions: governance, staking, and delegate manager contracts are deployed behind proxies (UUPS or Transparent), the initialize() function...
cards.proofShape: governance proxy initialize() can be called again

cards.title: 4. DAO Module Oracle Self-Answer Attack
cards.detect: When a DAO uses Gnosis Safe Modules with Reality.
check: VERIFY: When a DAO uses Gnosis Safe Modules with Reality.io oracle for proposal validation, the security depends on the oracle bond economics.
PROVE: SafeSnap DAO Module allows anyone to add proposals
cards.antipattern: SafeSnap DAO Module allows anyone to add proposals
cards.validWhen: When a DAO uses Gnosis Safe Modules with Reality.io oracle for proposal validation, the security depends on the oracle bond economics.
preconditions: a DAO uses Gnosis Safe Modules with Reality.io oracle for proposal validation, the security depends on the oracle bond economics.
cards.proofShape: SafeSnap DAO Module allows anyone to add proposals

cards.title: 5. Governance Treasury Drain via Token Acquisition
cards.detect: When governance token supply is small or concentrated, and the governance system has no minimum quorum percentage...
check: VERIFY: When governance token supply is small or concentrated, and the governance system has no minimum quorum percentage relative to total supply, an...
PROVE: No meaningful quorum — single holder can pass proposals
cards.antipattern: No meaningful quorum — single holder can pass proposals
cards.validWhen: When governance token supply is small or concentrated, and the governance system has no minimum quorum percentage relative to total supply, an attacker can acquire...
preconditions: governance token supply is small or concentrated, and the governance system has no minimum quorum percentage relative to total...
cards.proofShape: No meaningful quorum — single holder can pass proposals

cards.title: 1. 51% Attack via Arbitrary Execution
cards.detect: Governance systems often designate certain assets as "precious" or "protected" - they shouldn't be transferable through...
check: VERIFY: Signal 1: Critical input parameter not validated against expected range or format; Signal 2: Oracle data consumed without staleness check or sanity...
PROVE: DAO owns 100 ETH worth of "precious" NFTs (voting power NFTs); Governance is designed to protect these from being transferred
FALSIFY: Not this bug when: Validation exists but is in an upstream function caller; Safe if: Parameter range is inherently bounded by the type or protocol invariant
IMPACT: Complete bypass of asset protection mechanisms
cards.antipattern: Higher threshold but still allows arbitrary execution
cards.securePattern: Verify precious assets still owned after execution
cards.validWhen: Signal 1: Critical input parameter not validated against expected range or format; Signal 2: Oracle data consumed without staleness check or sanity bounds
cards.invalidWhen: Not this bug when: Validation exists but is in an upstream function caller; Safe if: Parameter range is inherently bounded by the type or protocol invariant
cards.impact: Complete bypass of asset protection mechanisms
preconditions: governance allows arbitrary proposal execution (arbitrary target addresses and calldata), a 51% majority can execute any...
attacker control of: specific conditions per pattern
Attacker acquires 51% voting power temporarily
Attacker creates proposal: target=preciousNFT, calldata=transfer(attacker, tokenId)
Attacker now owns the NFTs AND 51% of voting power
cards.proofShape: DAO owns 100 ETH worth of "precious" NFTs (voting power NFTs) -> Governance is designed to protect these from being transferred -> Attacker acquires 51% voting power temporarily
falsePositiveSignals: Not this bug when: Validation exists but is in an upstream function caller
Safe if: Parameter range is inherently bounded by the type or protocol invariant
Requires attacker control of: specific conditions per pattern

cards.title: 2. Loss of Veto Power Enabling Takeover
cards.detect: Veto power transfer or renunciation is not adequately protected:
check: VERIFY: Veto power transfer or renunciation is not adequately protected
PROVE: DAO has guardian with veto power to block malicious proposals; 51% attackers create proposal to set guardian = address(0)
FALSIFY: Guardian changes require timelock AND guardian approval
ANTIPATTERN: Guardian can be removed through governance
cards.antipattern: Guardian can be removed through governance
cards.securePattern: Guardian changes require timelock AND guardian approval
cards.validWhen: Veto power transfer or renunciation is not adequately protected
cards.invalidWhen: Guardian changes require timelock AND guardian approval
preconditions: 51% attackers create proposal to set guardian = address(0)
Attackers now have unrestricted 51% control
cards.proofShape: DAO has guardian with veto power to block malicious proposals -> 51% attackers create proposal to set guardian = address(0) -> Guardian cannot veto their own removal (would need to veto everything)

cards.title: 3. Centralized Governance Control
cards.detect: Protocol design concentrates too much power in single addresses:
check: VERIFY: Protocol design concentrates too much power in single addresses
PROVE: Protocol has single gov address with unlimited power; Gov address private key is compromised
FALSIFY: Critical functions require multi-sig + timelock
IMPACT: Single key compromise = total protocol compromise
cards.antipattern: Single address has total control
cards.securePattern: Critical functions require multi-sig + timelock
cards.validWhen: Protocol design concentrates too much power in single addresses
cards.invalidWhen: Critical functions require multi-sig + timelock
cards.impact: Single key compromise = total protocol compromise
preconditions: Attacker mints unlimited tokens, drains treasury
cards.proofShape: Protocol has single gov address with unlimited power -> Gov address private key is compromised -> Attacker mints unlimited tokens, drains treasury

cards.title: 4. Emergency Function Abuse
cards.detect: Emergency functions bypass normal governance security:
check: VERIFY: Emergency functions bypass normal governance security
PROVE: Users stake tokens for governance voting power; Tokens are supposed to be locked during active proposals
FALSIFY: Emergency withdraw checks for active governance
ANTIPATTERN: Emergency withdraw available during voting
cards.antipattern: Emergency withdraw available during voting
cards.securePattern: Emergency withdraw checks for active governance
cards.validWhen: Emergency functions bypass normal governance security
cards.invalidWhen: Emergency withdraw checks for active governance
cards.proofShape: Users stake tokens for governance voting power -> Tokens are supposed to be locked during active proposals -> Emergency withdraw function exists for "emergencies

cards.title: 5. Unrestricted Deployment/Election Takeover
cards.detect: No access control on critical deployment or election functions:
check: VERIFY: No access control on critical deployment or election functions
PROVE: Governance uses modular system with deployable "election masters; No restriction on who can deploy new election master
FALSIFY: Only pre-approved bytecode can be deployed
ANTIPATTERN: Anyone can deploy new election master
cards.antipattern: Anyone can deploy new election master
cards.securePattern: Only pre-approved bytecode can be deployed
cards.validWhen: No access control on critical deployment or election functions
cards.invalidWhen: Only pre-approved bytecode can be deployed
preconditions: Attacker deploys malicious election master
Malicious master declares attacker as winner
Attacker gains governance control
cards.proofShape: Governance uses modular system with deployable "election masters -> No restriction on who can deploy new election master -> Attacker deploys malicious election master

cards.title: 1. Unrestricted Proposal Cancellation
cards.detect: The `cancel()` function lacks proper access control, allowing anyone (or a broader class of users than intended) to...
check: VERIFY: Signal 1: Critical input parameter not validated against expected range or format; Signal 2: Oracle data consumed without staleness check or sanity...
PROVE: Legitimate proposal passes voting and is queued for execution; Attacker (or even proposer's own tokens sold) calls cancel()
FALSIFY: Not this bug when: Validation exists but is in an upstream function caller; Safe if: Parameter range is inherently bounded by the type or protocol invariant
IMPACT: Proposals can be cancelled by unauthorized parties
cards.antipattern: Uses current voting power, not snapshot
cards.securePattern: Check voting power at proposal creation snapshot
cards.validWhen: Signal 1: Critical input parameter not validated against expected range or format; Signal 2: Oracle data consumed without staleness check or sanity bounds
cards.invalidWhen: Not this bug when: Validation exists but is in an upstream function caller; Safe if: Parameter range is inherently bounded by the type or protocol invariant
cards.impact: Proposals can be cancelled by unauthorized parties
preconditions: attacker control of: specific conditions per pattern
Attacker (or even proposer's own tokens sold) calls cancel()
Attacker repeats for every proposal, griefing entire governance
cards.proofShape: Legitimate proposal passes voting and is queued for execution -> Attacker (or even proposer's own tokens sold) calls cancel() -> Proposal is cancelled, negating all voting efforts
falsePositiveSignals: Not this bug when: Validation exists but is in an upstream function caller
Safe if: Parameter range is inherently bounded by the type or protocol invariant
Requires attacker control of: specific conditions per pattern

cards.title: 2. Proposal Threshold Bypass for Griefing
cards.detect: Threshold is checked at proposal creation time using current voting power.
check: VERIFY: Threshold is checked at proposal creation time using current voting power.
PROVE: Proposal threshold: 1% of total supply (10,000 tokens); Attacker flash loans 10,000 tokens
FALSIFY: Guardian can cancel spam proposals
ANTIPATTERN: Only checks threshold at creation, no ongoing requirement
cards.antipattern: Only checks threshold at creation, no ongoing requirement
cards.securePattern: Guardian can cancel spam proposals
cards.validWhen: Threshold is checked at proposal creation time using current voting power.
cards.invalidWhen: Guardian can cancel spam proposals
preconditions: Proposal threshold: 1% of total supply (10,000 tokens)
Attacker flash loans 10,000 tokens
Attacker creates spam proposal
Attacker repays flash loan in same transaction
cards.proofShape: Proposal threshold: 1% of total supply (10,000 tokens) -> Attacker flash loans 10,000 tokens -> Attacker creates spam proposal

cards.title: 3. Proposal Expiration Logic Errors
cards.detect: Expiration timestamp calculation errors:
check: VERIFY: Expiration timestamp calculation errors
PROVE: Proposal passes voting on day 1; Grace period: 7 days to execute
FALSIFY: Proper expiration based on voting end time
ANTIPATTERN: Uses startTime instead of endTime
cards.antipattern: Uses startTime instead of endTime
cards.securePattern: Proper expiration based on voting end time
cards.validWhen: Expiration timestamp calculation errors
cards.invalidWhen: Proper expiration based on voting end time
cards.proofShape: Proposal passes voting on day 1 -> Grace period: 7 days to execute -> Bug: Expiration = startTime + gracePeriod (should be endTime + gracePeriod)

cards.title: 4. State Machine Transition Vulnerabilities
cards.detect:
State machine implementation errors:

- Missing state checks before transitions

- Wrong state returned from state()...
check: VERIFY: State machine implementation errors
PROVE: Can execute without going through queue
FALSIFY: Proper state machine with all transitions validated
cards.antipattern: Can execute without going through queue
cards.securePattern: Proper state machine with all transitions validated
cards.validWhen: State machine implementation errors
cards.invalidWhen: Proper state machine with all transitions validated
cards.proofShape: Can execute without going through queue

cards.title: 5. Proposal Execution Vulnerabilities
cards.detect:
Execution-related issues:

- Proposal content modified after creation

- Execution without proper state checks

-...
check: VERIFY: Execution-related issues
PROVE: Proposal data not immutable
FALSIFY: Proposal content stored by hash
cards.antipattern: Proposal data not immutable
cards.securePattern: Proposal content stored by hash
cards.validWhen: Execution-related issues
cards.invalidWhen: Proposal content stored by hash
cards.proofShape: Proposal data not immutable

cards.title: 1. Quorum Lowering via Delegation Abuse
cards.detect: Quorum is calculated based on total adjusted supply at a snapshot block.
check: VERIFY: Signal 1: Critical input parameter not validated against expected range or format; Signal 2: Oracle data consumed without staleness check or sanity...
PROVE: Proposal starts with 100 ETH total adjusted supply; Quorum = 20% of adjusted supply = 20 ETH required
FALSIFY: Not this bug when: Validation exists but is in an upstream function caller; Safe if: Parameter range is inherently bounded by the type or protocol invariant
IMPACT: Quorum requirement can be artificially lowered
cards.antipattern: Adjusted supply can change after proposal creation
cards.securePattern: Quorum based on total token supply, not adjusted
cards.validWhen: Signal 1: Critical input parameter not validated against expected range or format; Signal 2: Oracle data consumed without staleness check or sanity bounds
cards.invalidWhen: Not this bug when: Validation exists but is in an upstream function caller; Safe if: Parameter range is inherently bounded by the type or protocol invariant
cards.impact: Quorum requirement can be artificially lowered
preconditions: attacker control of: specific conditions per pattern
Proposal starts with 100 ETH total adjusted supply
Quorum = 20% of adjusted supply = 20 ETH required
Group delegates 50 ETH to addresses that "don't count" as adjusted supply
Adjusted supply drops to 50 ETH
cards.proofShape: Proposal starts with 100 ETH total adjusted supply -> Quorum = 20% of adjusted supply = 20 ETH required -> Coordinated group holds 15 ETH of voting power
falsePositiveSignals: Not this bug when: Validation exists but is in an upstream function caller
Safe if: Parameter range is inherently bounded by the type or protocol invariant
Requires attacker control of: specific conditions per pattern

cards.title: 2. Dynamic Quorum Manipulation
cards.detect: Automatic quorum adjustment algorithms that lower quorum when participation drops can be gamed by coordinated...
check: VERIFY: Automatic quorum adjustment algorithms that lower quorum when participation drops can be gamed by coordinated non-participation.
PROVE: Normal quorum: 40% of voting power; Attacker group controls 15% of tokens
FALSIFY: Quorum has a minimum floor
ANTIPATTERN: Quorum decreases based on low participation
cards.antipattern: Quorum decreases based on low participation
cards.securePattern: Quorum has a minimum floor
cards.validWhen: Automatic quorum adjustment algorithms that lower quorum when participation drops can be gamed by coordinated non-participation.
cards.invalidWhen: Quorum has a minimum floor
preconditions: Attacker group controls 15% of tokens
Attacker's 15% is now sufficient to pass any proposal
cards.proofShape: Normal quorum: 40% of voting power -> Attacker group controls 15% of tokens -> Group boycotts multiple proposals, reducing participation

cards.title: 3. Quorum Threshold Miscalculation
cards.detect: Mathematical errors in quorum calculation - using wrong base values, missing percentage conversions, or comparing...
check: VERIFY: Mathematical errors in quorum calculation - using wrong base values, missing percentage conversions, or comparing against wrong vote tallies.
PROVE: Intended quorum: 51% of total votes FOR to pass; Bug: Code checks if forVotes > totalVotes * 0.04 (4%)
FALSIFY: Proper majority calculation
IMPACT: Proposals pass without required support
cards.antipattern: Quorum checked against FOR votes only, not total participation
cards.securePattern: Proper majority calculation
cards.validWhen: Mathematical errors in quorum calculation - using wrong base values, missing percentage conversions, or comparing against wrong vote tallies.
cards.invalidWhen: Proper majority calculation
preconditions: Attacker with only 5% voting power can pass any proposal
cards.proofShape: Intended quorum: 51% of total votes FOR to pass -> Bug: Code checks if forVotes > totalVotes * 0.04 (4%) -> Attacker with only 5% voting power can pass any proposal

cards.title: 4. Quorum Not Enforced in State Transitions
cards.detect: The `state()` function determines proposal outcome but fails to check quorum in the success path, or quorum check is...
check: VERIFY: state() function determines proposal outcome but fails to check quorum in the success path, or quorum check is placed in wrong branch of conditional...
PROVE: Proposal receives 10 FOR votes, 5 AGAINST votes; Quorum requirement: 1000 votes
FALSIFY: Quorum enforced in state transitions
ANTIPATTERN: Quorum not checked for SUCCEEDED state
cards.antipattern: Quorum not checked for SUCCEEDED state
cards.securePattern: Quorum enforced in state transitions
cards.validWhen: The state() function determines proposal outcome but fails to check quorum in the success path, or quorum check is placed in wrong branch of conditional logic.
cards.invalidWhen: Quorum enforced in state transitions
cards.proofShape: Proposal receives 10 FOR votes, 5 AGAINST votes -> Quorum requirement: 1000 votes -> Bug: State function only checks forVotes > againstVotes

cards.title: 5. Tie Handling Vulnerabilities
cards.detect: Using `>=` instead of `>` when comparing FOR votes to AGAINST votes causes ties to pass.
check: VERIFY: Using >= instead of > when comparing FOR votes to AGAINST votes causes ties to pass.
PROVE: Proposal: Transfer 1M tokens to attacker address; Voting: 500,000 FOR, 500,000 AGAINST (tie)
FALSIFY: Explicit tie handling
ANTIPATTERN: Doesn't require strict majority
cards.antipattern: Doesn't require strict majority
cards.securePattern: Explicit tie handling
cards.validWhen: Using >= instead of > when comparing FOR votes to AGAINST votes causes ties to pass.
cards.invalidWhen: Explicit tie handling
preconditions: FOR and AGAINST votes are equal, the outcome should typically be rejection.
Proposal: Transfer 1M tokens to attacker address
Attacker receives tokens
cards.proofShape: Proposal: Transfer 1M tokens to attacker address -> Voting: 500,000 FOR, 500,000 AGAINST (tie) -> Bug: Code uses forVotes >= againstVotes

cards.title: 1. Timelock Bypass via Privileged Functions
cards.detect: Critical governance functions that control the timelock parameters (delay, governor, admin) can be called directly by...
check: VERIFY: Signal 1: Critical input parameter not validated against expected range or format; Signal 2: Oracle data consumed without staleness check or sanity...
PROVE: Governor/Admin key is compromised or turns malicious; Attacker calls setGovernor(attackerAddress) - takes effect IMMEDIATELY
FALSIFY: Not this bug when: Validation exists but is in an upstream function caller; Safe if: Parameter range is inherently bounded by the type or protocol invariant
IMPACT: Complete bypass of all timelock security guarantees
cards.antipattern: Governor can set new governor immediately
cards.securePattern: All administrative actions go through timelock
cards.validWhen: Signal 1: Critical input parameter not validated against expected range or format; Signal 2: Oracle data consumed without staleness check or sanity bounds
cards.invalidWhen: Not this bug when: Validation exists but is in an upstream function caller; Safe if: Parameter range is inherently bounded by the type or protocol invariant
cards.impact: Complete bypass of all timelock security guarantees
preconditions: attacker control of: specific conditions per pattern
Governor/Admin key is compromised or turns malicious
Attacker calls setGovernor(attackerAddress) - takes effect IMMEDIATELY
New attacker-controlled governor calls setDelay(0) - takes effect IMMEDIATELY
Attacker now has unrestricted access to all timelocked functions
cards.proofShape: Governor/Admin key is compromised or turns malicious -> Attacker calls setGovernor(attackerAddress) - takes effect IMMEDIATELY -> New attacker-controlled governor calls setDelay(0) - takes effect IMMEDIATELY
falsePositiveSignals: Not this bug when: Validation exists but is in an upstream function caller
Safe if: Parameter range is inherently bounded by the type or protocol invariant
Requires attacker control of: specific conditions per pattern

cards.title: 2. Zero/Minimal Delay Vulnerabilities
cards.detect: Constructor or initialization code sets the delay to zero, or validation logic allows zero/minimal delays, effectively...
check: VERIFY: Constructor or initialization code sets the delay to zero, or validation logic allows zero/minimal delays, effectively disabling the timelock.
PROVE: Protocol deploys timelock with delay = 0 (by accident or design flaw); Proposals can be executed immediately after creation
FALSIFY: Minimum delay enforced
ANTIPATTERN: Delay hardcoded to 0 despite parameter
cards.antipattern: Delay hardcoded to 0 despite parameter
cards.securePattern: Minimum delay enforced
cards.validWhen: Constructor or initialization code sets the delay to zero, or validation logic allows zero/minimal delays, effectively disabling the timelock.
cards.invalidWhen: Minimum delay enforced
cards.proofShape: Protocol deploys timelock with delay = 0 (by accident or design flaw) -> Proposals can be executed immediately after creation -> Malicious proposals cannot be detected or prevented

cards.title: 3. Timelock Reduction During Active Lock
cards.detect: Timelock end timestamp is calculated as `block.timestamp + timelockLength` every time a minting/locking function is...
check: VERIFY: Timelock end timestamp is calculated as block.timestamp + timelockLength every time a minting/locking function is called, rather than taking the...
PROVE: User locks tokens via StakingZap with 7-day timelock; User immediately deposits a tiny amount via a different path with 2ms timelock
FALSIFY: Only extend timelock, never reduce
ANTIPATTERN: Always overwrites timelock, even if shorter
cards.antipattern: Always overwrites timelock, even if shorter
cards.securePattern: Only extend timelock, never reduce
cards.validWhen: Timelock end timestamp is calculated as block.timestamp + timelockLength every time a minting/locking function is called, rather than taking the maximum of existing and...
cards.invalidWhen: Only extend timelock, never reduce
preconditions: a new timelock duration is applied retroactively to existing locks, users can reduce their lock periods by triggering...
User immediately deposits a tiny amount via a different path with 2ms timelock
cards.proofShape: User locks tokens via StakingZap with 7-day timelock -> User immediately deposits a tiny amount via a different path with 2ms timelock -> Timelock is recalculated: block.timestamp + 2ms

cards.title: 4. Canceled Proposal Still Executable
cards.detect: The `cancel()` function in the governance contract only updates the governance state but does not call `cancel()` on...
check: VERIFY: cancel() function in the governance contract only updates the governance state but does not call cancel() on the TimelockController, leaving the...
PROVE: Proposal is created and passes voting; Proposal is scheduled in TimelockController
FALSIFY: Cancel propagates to timelock
ANTIPATTERN: Cancel only updates governance state, not timelock
cards.antipattern: Cancel only updates governance state, not timelock
cards.securePattern: Cancel propagates to timelock
cards.validWhen: The cancel() function in the governance contract only updates the governance state but does not call cancel() on the TimelockController, leaving the operation still...
cards.invalidWhen: Cancel propagates to timelock
preconditions: a proposal is canceled in the governance contract but not canceled in the underlying timelock controller, the proposal remains...
cards.proofShape: Proposal is created and passes voting -> Proposal is scheduled in TimelockController -> Proposer or guardian cancels proposal in Governance contract

cards.title: 5. Storage Collision Attacks
cards.detect: The `cancel()` function takes a raw `bytes32` id as input without validation.
check: VERIFY: cancel() function takes a raw bytes32 id as input without validation.
PROVE: Attacker with CANCELLER_ROLE calls cancel(bytes32(0)); Storage slot for minimum delay is cleared (if it has LSB = 0)
FALSIFY: ID is computed, not provided directly
ANTIPATTERN: cancel() takes raw id, allowing storage manipulation
cards.antipattern: cancel() takes raw id, allowing storage manipulation
cards.securePattern: ID is computed, not provided directly
cards.validWhen: The cancel() function takes a raw bytes32 id as input without validation.
cards.invalidWhen: ID is computed, not provided directly
preconditions: Attacker with CANCELLER_ROLE calls cancel(bytes32(0))
Attacker reinitializes with themselves as admin with zero delay
cards.proofShape: Attacker with CANCELLER_ROLE calls cancel(bytes32(0)) -> Storage slot for minimum delay is cleared (if it has LSB = 0) -> Contract now has zero minimum delay

cards.title: 1. Double Voting via Delegation Abuse
cards.detect: When a user delegates their voting power and later withdraws/transfers their stake, there are no restrictions...
check: VERIFY: Signal 1: Critical input parameter not validated against expected range or format; Signal 2: Oracle data consumed without staleness check or sanity...
PROVE: Alice delegates her voting power (100 tokens) to Bob; Bob votes on a proposal with 100 + his own votes
FALSIFY: Not this bug when: Validation exists but is in an upstream function caller; Safe if: Parameter range is inherently bounded by the type or protocol invariant
IMPACT: Voting power can be multiplied arbitrarily
cards.antipattern: This is a storage reference - modifies previous checkpoint!
cards.securePattern: Use memory to read checkpoint, only write new one
cards.validWhen: Signal 1: Critical input parameter not validated against expected range or format; Signal 2: Oracle data consumed without staleness check or sanity bounds
cards.invalidWhen: Not this bug when: Validation exists but is in an upstream function caller; Safe if: Parameter range is inherently bounded by the type or protocol invariant
cards.impact: Voting power can be multiplied arbitrarily
preconditions: attacker control of: specific conditions per pattern
a user delegates their voting power and later withdraws/transfers their stake, there are no restrictions preventing the delegated...
cards.proofShape: Alice delegates her voting power (100 tokens) to Bob -> Bob votes on a proposal with 100 + his own votes -> Alice withdraws her stake during the voting period
falsePositiveSignals: Not this bug when: Validation exists but is in an upstream function caller
Safe if: Parameter range is inherently bounded by the type or protocol invariant
Requires attacker control of: specific conditions per pattern

cards.title: 2. Flash Loan Voting Attacks
cards.detect: Voting power is calculated based on current token balance at vote time, rather than a historical snapshot taken before...
check: VERIFY: Voting power is calculated based on current token balance at vote time, rather than a historical snapshot taken before the proposal was created.
PROVE: Attacker takes a flash loan of governance tokens; In the same transaction, attacker votes on a critical proposal
FALSIFY: Voting power based on snapshot at proposal creation
ANTIPATTERN: Voting allowed immediately after receiving tokens
cards.antipattern: Voting allowed immediately after receiving tokens
cards.securePattern: Voting power based on snapshot at proposal creation
cards.validWhen: Voting power is calculated based on current token balance at vote time, rather than a historical snapshot taken before the proposal was created.
cards.invalidWhen: Voting power based on snapshot at proposal creation
preconditions: Attacker takes a flash loan of governance tokens
In the same transaction, attacker votes on a critical proposal
Attacker returns flash loan
Proposal outcome is manipulated without real token ownership
cards.proofShape: Attacker takes a flash loan of governance tokens -> In the same transaction, attacker votes on a critical proposal -> Voting power is calculated based on current (borrowed) balance

cards.title: 3. Unlimited Vote Minting
cards.detect: Public initialization functions that mint voting tokens lack proper access control or single-use enforcement, allowing...
check: VERIFY: Public initialization functions that mint voting tokens lack proper access control or single-use enforcement, allowing repeated calls to inflate...
PROVE: Attacker calls initializeDistributionRecord() with valid merkle proof; Function mints voting tokens equal to their total claimable amount
FALSIFY: Only allow initialization once per beneficiary
ANTIPATTERN: Can be called multiple times to mint infinite votes
cards.antipattern: Can be called multiple times to mint infinite votes
cards.securePattern: Only allow initialization once per beneficiary
cards.validWhen: Public initialization functions that mint voting tokens lack proper access control or single-use enforcement, allowing repeated calls to inflate voting power.
cards.invalidWhen: Only allow initialization once per beneficiary
preconditions: Attacker calls initializeDistributionRecord() with valid merkle proof
Attacker calls the function again (and again)
Attacker now has unlimited voting power
cards.proofShape: Attacker calls initializeDistributionRecord() with valid merkle proof -> Function mints voting tokens equal to their total claimable amount -> Attacker calls the function again (and again)

cards.title: 4. Missing Voting Power Snapshots
cards.detect: The governance contract uses current voting power (`getVotingPower(msg.sender)`) instead of historical voting power at...
check: VERIFY: governance contract uses current voting power (getVotingPower(msg.sender)) instead of historical voting power at a specific snapshot block.
PROVE: User sees a proposal they want to pass/fail; User increases their locked tokens (or acquires more)
FALSIFY: Use snapshot from proposal creation
ANTIPATTERN: Uses current voting power, not snapshot
cards.antipattern: Uses current voting power, not snapshot
cards.securePattern: Use snapshot from proposal creation
cards.validWhen: The governance contract uses current voting power (getVotingPower(msg.sender)) instead of historical voting power at a specific snapshot block.
cards.invalidWhen: Use snapshot from proposal creation
preconditions: voting power is calculated at vote time rather than at proposal creation, it creates opportunities for manipulation by acquiring...
cards.proofShape: User sees a proposal they want to pass/fail -> User increases their locked tokens (or acquires more) -> User votes with inflated voting power

cards.title: 5. Delegation Logic Errors
cards.detect: Self-delegation toggles community power
check: VERIFY: Self-delegation toggles community power
cards.antipattern: Self-delegation toggles community power
cards.validWhen: Self-delegation toggles community power

cards.title: Vulnerability Title
cards.detect: DAO Governance Vulnerabilities** — Voting power manipulation, timelock bypass, proposal execution abuse, delegation...
check: VERIFY: Signal 1: castVote uses current balance (getVotes(block.number)) instead of snapshot at proposal creation (getPastVotes(proposalSnapshot)); Signal 2:...
FALSIFY: Not this bug when: Governance uses getPastVotes(proposalSnapshot) from OZ Governor, which snapshots at proposal creation; Safe if: Timelock's setDelay and setGovernor...
cards.validWhen: Signal 1: castVote uses current balance (getVotes(block.number)) instead of snapshot at proposal creation (getPastVotes(proposalSnapshot)); Signal 2: Quorum calculated...
cards.invalidWhen: Not this bug when: Governance uses getPastVotes(proposalSnapshot) from OZ Governor, which snapshots at proposal creation; Safe if: Timelock's setDelay and setGovernor...
falsePositiveSignals: Not this bug when: Governance uses getPastVotes(proposalSnapshot) from OZ Governor, which snapshots at proposal creation
Safe if: Timelock's setDelay and setGovernor can only be called via the timelock itself (require(msg.sender == address(this)))
Safe if: cancel() propagates cancellation to both Governor state and Timelock state

cards.title: Section 1: Flash Loan & Snapshot-Less Voting Power Manipulation
cards.detect: Governance contracts that use current token balance (instead of a historical snapshot at proposal creation) for voting...
check: VERIFY: Governance contracts that use current token balance (instead of a historical snapshot at proposal creation) for voting power allow attackers to...
PROVE: *Path A: Flash Loan Voting; Attacker takes a flash loan of governance tokens (or borrows from pool)
ANTIPATTERN: No access control on re-initialization — unlimited voting power mint
cards.antipattern: No access control on re-initialization — unlimited voting power mint
cards.validWhen: Governance contracts that use current token balance (instead of a historical snapshot at proposal creation) for voting power allow attackers to temporarily inflate their...
preconditions: Attacker takes a flash loan of governance tokens (or borrows from pool)
Attacker calls castVote() with enormous voting weight
Attacker repays flash loan in same transaction
User increases lock/deposit → votes with inflated power → immediately withdraws
cards.proofShape: *Path A: Flash Loan Voting -> Attacker takes a flash loan of governance tokens (or borrows from pool) -> Attacker calls castVote() with enormous voting weight

cards.title: Section 2: Quorum Manipulation & Threshold Bypass
cards.detect: Quorum thresholds calculated dynamically from current total supply (instead of frozen at proposal creation) can be...
check: VERIFY: Quorum thresholds calculated dynamically from current total supply (instead of frozen at proposal creation) can be manipulated by coordinated token...
PROVE: *Path A: Quorum Lowered via Token Withdrawal; Attacker coordinates with group to lock tokens and create a proposal
ANTIPATTERN: `4` means 4%, NOT 1/4th (25%) — proposals pass at 6.25x lower threshold
cards.antipattern: `4` means 4%, NOT 1/4th (25%) — proposals pass at 6.25x lower threshold
cards.validWhen: Quorum thresholds calculated dynamically from current total supply (instead of frozen at proposal creation) can be manipulated by coordinated token burns, withdrawals,...
preconditions: Attacker coordinates with group to lock tokens and create a proposal
Attacker's votes now exceed lowered quorum → proposal passes
Proposals pass with only 4% of supply supporting
Attacker delegates to self, then creates a proposal → quorum locked at reduced total
cards.proofShape: *Path A: Quorum Lowered via Token Withdrawal -> Attacker coordinates with group to lock tokens and create a proposal -> After proposal enters voting period, coordinated group withdraws locks (after expiry)

cards.title: Section 3: Timelock Bypass & Unauthorized Execution
cards.detect: Timelocks are meant to enforce a mandatory delay between proposal approval and execution.
check: VERIFY: Timelocks are meant to enforce a mandatory delay between proposal approval and execution.
PROVE: *Path A: Governor Sets Delay to Zero; Governor calls Timelock.setGovernor(attacker) — effective immediately (no timelock enforcement)
ANTIPATTERN: setGovernor effective immediately — no timelock enforcement
cards.antipattern: setGovernor effective immediately — no timelock enforcement
cards.validWhen: Timelocks are meant to enforce a mandatory delay between proposal approval and execution.
preconditions: Governor calls Timelock.setGovernor(attacker) — effective immediately (no timelock enforcement)
Attacker (now governor) calls Timelock.setDelay(0) — effective immediately
Attacker can now execute any operation instantly, including minting unlimited tokens
cards.proofShape: *Path A: Governor Sets Delay to Zero -> Governor calls Timelock.setGovernor(attacker) — effective immediately (no timelock enforcement) -> Attacker (now governor) calls Timelock.setDelay(0) — effective immediately

cards.title: Section 4: Delegation & Checkpoint Accounting Bugs
cards.detect: Vote delegation and checkpoint systems track historical voting power for governance snapshots.
check: VERIFY: Vote delegation and checkpoint systems track historical voting power for governance snapshots.
PROVE: *Path A: _writeCheckpoint Memory vs Storage Bug; User delegates tokenA to userB, then delegates tokenA to userC in same block
ANTIPATTERN: No stake lockup during active voting — delegate→vote→withdraw→re-vote
cards.antipattern: No stake lockup during active voting — delegate→vote→withdraw→re-vote
cards.validWhen: Vote delegation and checkpoint systems track historical voting power for governance snapshots.
preconditions: Only first delegation persists — second is silently lost
Attacker creates 500 minimum-value lock NFTs, delegates all to victim
cards.proofShape: *Path A: _writeCheckpoint Memory vs Storage Bug -> User delegates tokenA to userB, then delegates tokenA to userC in same block -> _writeCheckpoint reads oldCheckpoint as memory (not storage)

cards.title: Section 5: Proposal Cancellation & Execution Abuse
cards.detect: Proposal lifecycle functions (`cancel`, `execute`, `queue`) with insufficient state validation allow: executing...
check: VERIFY: Proposal lifecycle functions (cancel, execute, queue) with insufficient state validation allow: executing defeated/expired proposals, canceling...
PROVE: *Path A: Execute Defeated/Expired Proposals; execute() accepts Succeeded, Defeated, and Expired states (too permissive)
ANTIPATTERN: Can cancel in all states except Executed — blocks governance
cards.antipattern: Can cancel in all states except Executed — blocks governance
cards.validWhen: Proposal lifecycle functions (cancel, execute, queue) with insufficient state validation allow: executing defeated/expired proposals, canceling succeeded proposals to...
preconditions: Attacker front-runs legitimate proposal execution with an expired proposal
Target function has one-time-per-period guard → attacker's execution blocks legitimate one
Mints new governance NFT with astronomical voting power to attacker
Attacker now has 100% voting power → creates unanimously-voted proposal
cards.proofShape: *Path A: Execute Defeated/Expired Proposals -> execute() accepts Succeeded, Defeated, and Expired states (too permissive) -> Attacker front-runs legitimate proposal execution with an expired proposal

cards.title: Section 6: Quadratic Voting & Tally Errors
cards.detect: Quadratic voting systems that fail to properly square individual votes before summing them, or that don't enforce...
check: VERIFY: Quadratic voting systems that fail to properly square individual votes before summing them, or that don't enforce majority requirements (ties pass),...
PROVE: *Path A: Quadratic Sum Not Squared; Spec says: sum of squares of votes ≤ square of token holdings
ANTIPATTERN: votes not squared before summing — lower hijack threshold
cards.antipattern: votes not squared before summing — lower hijack threshold
cards.validWhen: Quadratic voting systems that fail to properly square individual votes before summing them, or that don't enforce majority requirements (ties pass), produce outcomes...
preconditions: Attacker concentrates all votes on one proposal instead of distributing
cards.proofShape: *Path A: Quadratic Sum Not Squared -> Spec says: sum of squares of votes ≤ square of token holdings -> Code enforces: sum of absolute votes ≤ square of holdings (votes NOT squared)

cards.title: Section 7: Multisig & Safe Module Exploits
cards.detect: Gnosis Safe modules and signer gate contracts assume module counts remain consistent and that hat owners are always...
check: VERIFY: Gnosis Safe modules and signer gate contracts assume module counts remain consistent and that hat owners are always valid addresses.
PROVE: *Path A: Module Count Desync Bricks Safe; External module calls Safe.enableModule() directly (bypasses enableNewModule())
ANTIPATTERN: External module adds module → enabledModuleCount desync → safe bricked
cards.antipattern: External module adds module → enabledModuleCount desync → safe bricked
cards.validWhen: Gnosis Safe modules and signer gate contracts assume module counts remain consistent and that hat owners are always valid addresses.
cards.proofShape: *Path A: Module Count Desync Bricks Safe -> External module calls Safe.enableModule() directly (bypasses enableNewModule()) -> enabledModuleCount not incremented → guard hash check fails

cards.title: Vulnerability Title
cards.detect: veToken / Voting Escrow Governance Vulnerabilities** — Comprehensive patterns across Solidly/ve(3,3)-style governance...
check: VERIFY: Signal 1: Bribe.withdraw() does not decrement totalVoting while Bribe.deposit() does increment it; Signal 2: claimBribes/getRewardForOwner does not...
FALSIFY: Not this bug when: Protocol uses time-weighted voting with automatic decay in Bribe deposits; Safe if: Epoch guard modifier (onlyNewEpoch) is applied to all...
cards.validWhen: Signal 1: Bribe.withdraw() does not decrement totalVoting while Bribe.deposit() does increment it; Signal 2: claimBribes/getRewardForOwner does not check if the tokenId...
cards.invalidWhen: Not this bug when: Protocol uses time-weighted voting with automatic decay in Bribe deposits; Safe if: Epoch guard modifier (onlyNewEpoch) is applied to all...
preconditions: attacker control of: veNFT (lock/vote timing), or ability to call poke() repeatedly, or knowledge of epoch boundary timing
falsePositiveSignals: Not this bug when: Protocol uses time-weighted voting with automatic decay in Bribe deposits
Safe if: Epoch guard modifier (onlyNewEpoch) is applied to all vote/poke/claim paths
Safe if: totalVoting is symmetrically updated in both deposit() and withdraw()

cards.title: Section 1: Bribe Reward Theft & Epoch Accounting Exploits
cards.detect: Bribe contracts distribute rewards based on voting checkpoints recorded per epoch.
check: VERIFY: Bribe contracts distribute rewards based on voting checkpoints recorded per epoch.
PROVE: *Path A: Claiming Bribes for Unvoted Epochs; Alice votes in epoch 1, Bob votes in epoch 1
ANTIPATTERN: deposit increments totalVoting, withdraw does not decrement it
cards.antipattern: deposit increments totalVoting, withdraw does not decrement it
cards.validWhen: Bribe contracts distribute rewards based on voting checkpoints recorded per epoch.
preconditions: Alice calls claimBribes for epoch 2 — she did NOT vote in epoch 2 but getRewardForOwner reads her checkpoint balance from the...
Bribe.deposit() increments both balanceOf[tokenId] and totalVoting, writes voting checkpoint
Attacker repeatedly calls poke() which triggers withdraw → deposit cycles
Each cycle: withdraw doesn't reduce totalVoting, but the new deposit checkpoint records lowered balance
Attacker exploits deflated totalVoting to claim disproportionate bribe rewards
cards.proofShape: *Path A: Claiming Bribes for Unvoted Epochs -> Alice votes in epoch 1, Bob votes in epoch 1 -> Epoch 2 starts — Alice claims epoch 1 bribes correctly

cards.title: Section 2: Voting Power Manipulation & Lock Accounting Bugs
cards.detect: VotingEscrow contracts manage veNFT positions with lock amount and duration tracking.
check: VERIFY: VotingEscrow contracts manage veNFT positions with lock amount and duration tracking.
PROVE: *Path A: Double Voting Power via Lock Increase; User creates a lock with a small initial amount (e.g., 1 wei)
ANTIPATTERN: amount is double-counted — added in increaseLock AND in calculateAndUpdatePower
cards.antipattern: amount is double-counted — added in increaseLock AND in calculateAndUpdatePower
cards.validWhen: VotingEscrow contracts manage veNFT positions with lock amount and duration tracking.
cards.proofShape: *Path A: Double Voting Power via Lock Increase -> User creates a lock with a small initial amount (e.g., 1 wei) -> User calls increase() with the full amount they want to lock

cards.title: Section 3: Gauge Voting & Emission Distribution Exploits
cards.detect: GaugeController contracts allow users to allocate voting weight across gauges to direct token emissions.
check: VERIFY: GaugeController contracts allow users to allocate voting weight across gauges to direct token emissions.
PROVE: *Path A: Gauge Over-Voting (No Cumulative Weight Tracking); GaugeController.vote() allows setting weight per gauge but doesn't track total weight across all gauges
ANTIPATTERN: No check that total user weight across all gauges <= WEIGHT_PRECISION
cards.antipattern: No check that total user weight across all gauges <= WEIGHT_PRECISION
cards.validWhen: GaugeController contracts allow users to allocate voting weight across gauges to direct token emissions.
preconditions: Admin calls killGauge() — sets isAlive[gauge] = false
cards.proofShape: *Path A: Gauge Over-Voting (No Cumulative Weight Tracking) -> GaugeController.vote() allows setting weight per gauge but doesn't track total weight across all gauges -> User votes 100% weight on gauge A, then 100%...

cards.title: Section 5: Boost Mechanism Exploits
cards.detect: Boost controllers calculate reward multipliers based on veToken holdings.
check: VERIFY: Boost controllers calculate reward multipliers based on veToken holdings.
PROVE: *Path A: Unbacked Boost via Withdraw After Delegation; User has veTokens and calls delegateBoost(to, amount, duration)
ANTIPATTERN: Math simplifies to always returning MAX_BOOST for any nonzero amount
cards.antipattern: Math simplifies to always returning MAX_BOOST for any nonzero amount
cards.validWhen: Boost controllers calculate reward multipliers based on veToken holdings.
cards.proofShape: *Path A: Unbacked Boost via Withdraw After Delegation -> User has veTokens and calls delegateBoost(to, amount, duration) -> Contract checks user's veToken balance ≥ amount at delegation time

cards.title: Section 6: Reward & Emission Distribution Bugs
cards.detect: Minter → Voter → Gauge reward pipelines can fail when: distribution functions don't track per-gauge amounts already...
check: VERIFY: Minter → Voter → Gauge reward pipelines can fail when: distribution functions don't track per-gauge amounts already sent, epoch ordering between...
PROVE: *Path A: Repeated distributeEx Drains All Rewards; distributeEx() distributes rewards to gauges based on weight ratios
ANTIPATTERN: No tracking of already-distributed amounts per gauge per epoch
cards.antipattern: No tracking of already-distributed amounts per gauge per epoch
cards.validWhen: Minter → Voter → Gauge reward pipelines can fail when: distribution functions don't track per-gauge amounts already sent, epoch ordering between minter.updatePeriod()...
preconditions: Attacker calls distributeEx() with only their gauge repeatedly
*Path B: First-Epoch Emissions Stuck Forever
distribute() is called before minter.updatePeriod() in the first epoch
Impact: Permanent loss of first-epoch rewards
cards.proofShape: *Path A: Repeated distributeEx Drains All Rewards -> distributeEx() distributes rewards to gauges based on weight ratios -> If a gauge's notifyRewardAmount() returns false, claimable is added back

cards.title: Section 7: Checkpoint & Voting Power Decay Bugs
cards.detect: VotingEscrow uses checkpoint systems to record point-in-time voting power that decays linearly over lock duration.
check: VERIFY: VotingEscrow uses checkpoint systems to record point-in-time voting power that decays linearly over lock duration.
PROVE: *Path A: Checkpoint Block Number Never Updates; Block slope calculation: blockSlope = (MULTIPLIER * (block.number - lastPoint.blk)) / (block.timestamp - lastPoint.ts)
ANTIPATTERN: MULTIPLIER=2 is too small — blockSlope always rounds to zero
cards.antipattern: MULTIPLIER=2 is too small — blockSlope always rounds to zero
cards.validWhen: VotingEscrow uses checkpoint systems to record point-in-time voting power that decays linearly over lock duration.
preconditions: *Path B: Non-Decaying Bribe Deposits
User votes once after creating lock — Bribe records full veNFT balance as deposit amount
veNFT balance decays linearly over time, but Bribe deposit does NOT auto-decay
User avoids re-voting in subsequent epochs to keep inflated deposit weight
cards.proofShape: *Path A: Checkpoint Block Number Never Updates -> Block slope calculation: blockSlope = (MULTIPLIER * (block.number - lastPoint.blk)) / (block.timestamp - lastPoint.ts) -> MULTIPLIER = 2, block time ~12 seconds →...

cards.title: Section 8: Delegation & NFT Operation Exploits
cards.detect: veNFT-based systems allow delegation, merging, and managed deposits.
check: VERIFY: veNFT-based systems allow delegation, merging, and managed deposits.
PROVE: *Path A: MAX_DELEGATES DoS; Attacker creates 1024 (MAX_DELEGATES) small veNFTs and delegates them all to a target
ANTIPATTERN: Extending expired position uses wrong dailyUnlockedAmounts key
cards.antipattern: Extending expired position uses wrong dailyUnlockedAmounts key
cards.validWhen: veNFT-based systems allow delegation, merging, and managed deposits.
preconditions: Attacker creates 1024 (MAX_DELEGATES) small veNFTs and delegates them all to a target
RewardsDistributor.claim() sends rewards via depositFor if lock is active, or transfers directly if expired
depositManaged() changes lock state such that the expiry check passes for non-expired locks
User calls depositManaged → claim extracts unvested VELO as direct transfer
cards.proofShape: *Path A: MAX_DELEGATES DoS -> Attacker creates 1024 (MAX_DELEGATES) small veNFTs and delegates them all to a target -> Target's transfer/withdraw operations must iterate through all delegations

cards.title: Vulnerability Title
cards.detect: Stablecoins are treated as permanently pegged to $1 (or to another stablecoin) without real-time price validation.
check: VERIFY: Signal 1: State variable updated after external interaction instead of before (CEI violation); Signal 2: Withdrawal path produces different...
PROVE: A stablecoin depegs below $1 (or above $1) due to market stress.; The protocol still values the stablecoin at $1 (or at the wrong reference asset) in pricing, minting,...
FALSIFY: Not this bug when: Standard security patterns (access control, reentrancy guards, input validation) are in place; Safe if: Protocol behavior matches documented...
IMPACT: Incorrect pricing of collateral and mint/redeem operations during depeg events.
Verify all stablecoin valuations use an oracle or validated peg tolerance.
Confirm mint/redeem logic handles depeg events and reverts when out of bounds.
cards.antipattern: Mints 1:1 without checking decimals or depeg risk
cards.securePattern: Use price feed with a tight peg tolerance and staleness checks
cards.validWhen: Signal 1: State variable updated after external interaction instead of before (CEI violation); Signal 2: Withdrawal path produces different accounting than deposit path...
cards.invalidWhen: Not this bug when: Standard security patterns (access control, reentrancy guards, input validation) are in place; Safe if: Protocol behavior matches documented...
cards.impact: Incorrect pricing of collateral and mint/redeem operations during depeg events.
preconditions: attacker control of: specific conditions per pattern
An attacker deposits depegged stablecoins or triggers mint/redeem flows to receive inflated value.
cards.proofShape: A stablecoin depegs below $1 (or above $1) due to market stress.
falsePositiveSignals: Not this bug when: Standard security patterns (access control, reentrancy guards, input validation) are in place
Safe if: Protocol behavior matches documented specification
Requires attacker control of: specific conditions per pattern
